instagram_caption:
The American Robin is one of the most recognizable songbirds across North America, easily spotted by its warm orange-red breast and cheerful, rolling song that often marks the first hint of spring. Despite being common in backyards, parks, and gardens, robins live surprisingly complex lives. They are early risers, often the first birds singing before sunrise, and they rely heavily on sharp eyesight and hearing to detect earthworms moving just beneath the soil surface. Contrary to popular belief, robins do not actually hear worms moving underground, they watch and listen for subtle movements in the grass, tilting their heads to get a better look before striking with precision.

Robins are also remarkably adaptable. During the breeding season they favor insects and worms for their protein content, but as autumn arrives they shift almost entirely to a diet of berries and fruit, sometimes traveling in large nomadic flocks that follow food availability rather than a strict migratory path south. This flexibility helps explain why some robins stay put through mild winters while others travel hundreds of miles depending on where the fruit is ripening that season.

Female robins build sturdy cup shaped nests from grass, twigs, and mud, often reusing the same territory year after year, and can raise two to three broods in a single season. Have you spotted a robin pulling a worm from your lawn this week? Next time you hear that familiar cheerful song at dawn, take a moment to appreciate just how tuned in these everyday backyard birds really are to the rhythms of the world around them.

video_prompt: A close-up shot of an American robin standing still on green grass, its feathers catching soft morning light. The bird tilts its head gently to one side as if listening closely, then remains poised and alert. Subtle, natural motion only: a slight ruffle of feathers in a light breeze, gentle head movement, calm ambient park background slightly out of focus. Photorealistic, warm natural lighting, shallow depth of field. No text overlays, no logos, no watermarks, keep the frame clean and focused entirely on the bird's natural, unscripted behavior.
alt_text: An American robin standing on green grass in soft morning light, head tilted as if listening for movement underground.
research_summary: The American robin (Turdus migratorius) is a migratory songbird native to North America, well known for its orange-red breast and rolling song, commonly found in gardens, parks, and lawns.
